speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Glen Gilder took place at his home not far from the Spring Valley Reservoir outside of Troy on December 9th, 1976. His wife Agnes Gilder also took part in the conversation. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they active when you were a kid?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, very.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yes, when I was a kid they were. But you know, they're kind of old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They're not.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You see, we had a good organization in Derry, and they had one here in Troy, but the membership just kept going, going, going, going. In Derry we were down to seven active members. Well, it's not enough. So we argued for two years, and finally we went in with Potlatch. And Troy argued for quite a period, and then they went all to Moscow. So, all the odd fellows here in town go to Moscow once in a while, Al Severson and Monty Helm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: oh, quite another. Art Fiddler.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first get interested in them?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, when I was a kid, I was pretty young. I was all fixed up to join the odd fellows along about 19, 19, with another group of young guys. And somehow or other, I got a job clear out of the country and had to go take it, so I didn't get in on that bunch. And then I didn't join until about 1965, probably, before I joined them. And I wouldn't have joined them then, but Carl Lancaster and Henry Jones and Dwight Corey, well, Carl and Henry were to go in with me in that first bunch. And they made up their mind by the guy that was going to get me in there, and they did it. But it was 1920 to 1965 before I did it. Now, those fellows had been in the lodge all that time.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I've seen one of the last papers where Eunice Jones was given a 50 -year pin at Pot Lodge.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, Edna had more years than that in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'll bet you that Edna – Butterfield?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I'll bet you that they joined along before the team got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They joined that lodge in – Well, did it say in that paper?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't think it did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a big, a major thing in the early years? Was it a really big work position?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You bet it was. You bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it compare to, like, the Grange?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It had much more percentage. It bought them much more percentage than the Grange does.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Take a little place like Princeton. And back during the early times, that Grange had around 70 members. And it dwindled down, down, down, and they went in with Potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Potlatch?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Princeton went to Potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the Grange?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, in the Oddfields. Oddfields. You're talking about –

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, it was strong. Not only here, but all over the United States. It had tremendous membership. In fact, there was Woodman, and the Redman, and the Masons, and the Oddfellows. And I think Oddfellows were – times were the highest of all, but I think there was times when they were – for long periods when they were second. The Redman I think have completely quit. The Woodman of the world have – I think have quit. And the Oddfellows, then, are probably in third place, second or third place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, with the Oddfellows, that was a lot bigger than the Grange at the time, wasn't it, when you were growing up?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A bigger – I mean, around here. I think so.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, the Grange was pretty popular, too.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, a big percentage of the Oddfellows were Granges. Same then as they are now, here in Troy. There's Alfred and Art, Monty. They're all Oddfellows. There's a lot of overlap between the two. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what's the difference between – is there much difference between the Oddfellows and, like, the Masons as a group?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They have different policies.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know very little about the Masons, but their purposes have always been pretty parallel, I believe. They have fellowship to benefit everybody involved in it. All that good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you, Glen, when you started working out?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were here?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, I don't know, about twelve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the first work that you did out?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, driving horses for somebody, I suppose. Yeah, I think that's it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know. It was just natural for me to drive horses, be around horses, as anything could be. When I first started hauling wheat from Kennedy Ford into Palouse for my uncle and granddad, I couldn't more lift a sack of wheat than I could fly. Nobody else had to load me and unload me. But I could drive the horses, four of them. I didn't take a back seat there from any of the big fellas either.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Then from there I went to drive in, oh, one of the first jobs I had to drive in a greater team on a highway greater from Harvard to the hoodoos. Old Duff used to come get me every spring and take me up with him. I'd step up two weeks and we'd fix all the culverts and clean out the ditches and grade the roads. I'd go home and have about, oh, maybe fifty dollars. I didn't make much in them days. I think about two dollars in a half a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you quit school by that time?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No. If I need to, I'd take a week off.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I'd go back and I was going to catch it up, but I don't think I ever did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There was nothing uncommon. If you got a job, go do it. Lots of times we could get a job for driving a team and wagon putting gravel on the road. In the fall of the year we could get a couple of weeks' worth. All we had to do was drive a team back and forth. Somebody else loaded us and then somebody helped us unload the dump boards.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's what I would sooner do. That's what I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You would sooner do than sit in the classroom?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No. If I was sitting in the classroom, I don't know for sure, but I think I must have spent a lot of my time looking out the window.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know, but I think I must have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it true for you that, now I've heard a couple of people say that to do a man's work is really what they wanted to do as a boy. To be able to do a man's work was what they were looking forward to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that was true for you?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, it was true. To compete with a man seemed to be the height of our ambition.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We were lucky and could keep up with him. We thought we were pretty big. If we could catch one that was a little older or a little awkward and could surpass him or something, then we had to swell head. We was always saying.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Take two kids out shocking hay with a pitchfork.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They'd race from morning till night, try to get that road done quicker than the other guy.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I worked one year for Lindsay. He had me shocking and she'd come out in the field pretty early in the morning after she got housework done. Her and I would race till noon. She'd go and get dinner after about dishes done and all that. She'd be right back out there in Dogland if we didn't race then until she had to go milk. We'd go out and make fun of each other like a couple of kids.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: She was forty -five years older than I was, about seventeen or eighteen.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: She worked awful hard, seriously. They made it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pretty common to do what she did for the women, to get out and work?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It was really common the women got out and helped. Sometimes they eliminated the hired men altogether.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Many, many, many of them. They could drive horses as good as a man.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You bet the wives and daughters they were right out in the field.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They didn't live so long.

speaker: Agnes Gilder
words: They didn't live so long?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know, Amelia's still going good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Amelia who?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Lindsay. She wasn't the last time I saw her, about fifteen years ago. She wasn't going real good, but then she's still going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you figure that that was pretty rough on women to do that then? Agnes, is that what you think?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, I do.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They didn't get over it so good. Nothing looked like it, but they didn't.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: She worked her head off, that's for sure, and paid for it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Her big trouble is she got off so heavily. She was way overweight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then she was still working?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, no while she was still working she was right.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, right on to a good working order, I think.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I think she waited on 130, 40, 50, somewhere around there, but now she's over 200. Well, I didn't know her when she was younger. When she was about, well her kids were about five or six years old. She had a hard girl life too, she helped and then went out and did. She did. After they got, well started and established, she had Genevieve and Neom and some of them, but for a number of years she just didn't.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But if she wasn't alone in that, all the neighbours, the Hansons, the Hoggins, and all those people that were close to them, it was all the same thing, the Gregorys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it just necessity that dictated it, that that's what a woman would do, or was it more trying to get ahead?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, it was quite a bit of a necessity because times weren't a great deal better than they are in this day and age. You might have had less money, but it became just as hard as it did in the last few years. Well, they had to sell a couple of hogs or a calf or something to pay us fellows our wages for the help they got in harvest and haying.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Money was hard to get, but it wasn't easy. And they all were starting. They had places to pay for and outfits to pay for and learning to make.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's one thing though, in those days young men and women got a better start than they have any chance of having now. Oh yeah. Of course they were.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They're up to 150 ,000 now, maybe 15 ,000. They've taken care of the whole thing, the deal in those days, but it's pretty hard to get, just saying.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's funny that there's a lot less opportunity now than there used to be. I think there'd be a way you could still compete.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, up to this last year it has been possible, but boy I don't think anybody can get ahead now. Well, I... no. They're just awful lucky to be able to come out even.

speaker: Agnes Gilder
words: What year?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Back before 1928. If a man had $100 he could start farming in a good shape.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you serious? $100?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We rented 80 acres and I had a harvest, what I'd made and harvested.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And Harold had an equal amount and we bought a Ford car with it. And we went to work in the mill. Every time we'd get a paycheck we'd buy a horse or a calf or a harness or a plow or some damn thing.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And we got started. We didn't have any cash, we just... every time we could make a dollar...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We just applied it to some little thing that'd probably grow. And most of it did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't have to spend much of that money just to live on at that time?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh no, eggs and beans and spuds were cheap.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's about all we knew how to cook. We'd match them before we married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now is this your own place that you had at the time?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, we rented 80 acres from my grandmother.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Got a start there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We did pretty good for 27, 28 and 29. Made some pretty good headway. And then the depression came and we went the other way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the deal with your grandmother? Was it a certain amount of money you paid or what?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, we gave her $125 a year for that place.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: She tickled the desk to give it to us.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Just for rent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you grow a crop on it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, you bet. Good one. Our first crop on that place... 40 acres... I think it was only about 28 acres a week, but it made 42 bushels. And we got $2 .15 a bushel. And that was making darn good money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Had two years of that and then the depression hit and wheat was worth $0 .25 a bushel. That's a different story. That wouldn't pay for... ...any part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the stock that you say you were buying? Were you raising them up then? You said you'd buy a pig or a calf?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yes, we did. We'd buy little pigs, little calves. And they grow and... Actually, that's all the gain we made is about what they grew, but... ...even that was pretty good for that time.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh... It's a different world. I don't know.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I asked a lady in Wyoming last...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: What would cost to start ranching over there?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And she told me... ...it would cost a million dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...that's a good thing.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: If they could get ahold of the lease... ...if they could get ahold of the land that is the homestead and the home ranch... ...and then to get ahold of a lease and keep it up... ...keep up their water rights... ...and stock enough to stock it... ...and the machinery enough to take care of it... ...that that's about what it would cost. Well, a young guy can't start unless you got a dad or granddad... ...with money to put him on the place.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And it's the same in wheat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It doesn't sound like it was easy, though, even in the 20s when you were doing it. I mean, it wasn't a... It certainly seemed like an easy haul to build up.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It wasn't, but... ...we were talking it over before we leased this place... ...and said if we could get a job in the middle, which we thought we could... ...that was eight hours. But it wouldn't hurt us to go home and do chores after those eight hours... ...any more than it would if we drove a team on a plow all day... ...and then went in and done chores in the same way. No, it wasn't easy. It was long hours. And hard work.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It could have been a lot of going without, but we were young... ...and didn't know enough to go without anything. We wanted something like that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We went and done it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: What would you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...give me a for instance of what you'd do instead of that you couldn't go without?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, go take our girl to the show or... ...or go to a dance. It was probably a couple of dollars.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And now, it would cost you $25.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We didn't go without because we didn't... They didn't have things like they've got them now. The only thing you could do is go to a show or go to a dance... ...or go to a party in somebody's house. That's all it was.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: A couple of dollars was plenty for a weekend.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But that couple of dollars was so big then... ...that I don't know whether the 25 is any bigger now than that was. I don't know.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you really holding down two jobs at once when you were doing that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yes, that's exactly right. I had some plowing to do after I got home from the mill at 3 o 'clock if I was on the day shift. I'd go out and plow till 6 or 7.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And that's the way I got all the work done. After work, do it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It was a small place.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: By figuring and scheming, taking turns... ...and another thing we could do, we had fairly good horses. We could... ...when I traded with my uncle... ...he'd put my crop in and then take my horses over to his place for the summer. Well, all I had to do as far as putting in my crop then was to buy the seed.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And we did that one year.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I did the work, but I don't think I did the thinking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Scheming work and did a lot of thinking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why didn't you do the thinking, Glen?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know what you mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you didn't think about it enough?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, did you?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, you had to...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know. You had to scheme and plan...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: ...and wonder, how the devil are you going to make it? What are you going to do to make this come out right? What are you going to do to make that come out right?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I didn't always hit it right. I missed it a few times. But then... ...that's what I mean by thinking.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Man... I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there real choices that you had to make? I mean, you could have gone two or three different ways, I don't think?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you could have invested in different ways in the stock...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: ...or you could have lunged in and hit it, or you could have lunged in and missed it. You know, things then were like they are now. Cattle could be a good price this year, or hogs could be a good price this year. Which they were in those first few years in 27, 8 and 9 way. Hogs were clear up to 13 cents a pound.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: In 1930 and 31, they were balancing between 2 and 3 cents a pound. I had some of those.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, is that thinking or is that just rolling the dice?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, it seems to me like maybe it's a matter of luck.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, just rolling the dice. Anytime that you start farming, you start rolling the dice... ...and you never quit rolling the dice until you quit farming.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There isn't any way to quit.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Sometimes you can guess ahead, but... ...it's not all head work either. It's an equal amount of pure luck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did it take you to decide that you'd be better off getting out of that place?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, after 3 years it was going darn good, we thought we'd get a little bigger. We did, we got a little bigger. We got a little bigger and put the much produce on the market, I guess. That's why hogs went from 13 cents to 2, because everybody went with us.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, you're always, always reaching out, trying to boost yourself. Lift yourself up another notch.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Sometimes you do it, sometimes you didn't.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: When wheat was 25 cents a bushel, a sack would hold 2 bushels and a half. And it cost us 30 cents to get the sack.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And I think it cost 10 cents a sack to get it thrashed, that's 40 cents.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And your seed had cost you around $2 an acre.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And your work and your horse work went into it and finished out the crop, but even so, that wasn't enough money to break even.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, I don't know. When the depression could come, we did have a few cows to milk and fairly good ones.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And by working out after my crop was in or during the winter, if I could get work during the winter, I'd take it, but I generally couldn't. If I could cut around three quarters of a cord of wood a day, that was enough to eat on. That's about, for 10 years, that's about what we did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Never had any health or relief or WPA. We didn't have it. We could have got it, I presume. I never tried. I didn't want it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't blame the guys that did get it. I think it was more power to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So really then, it was just holding on, not trying to... The chance of getting ahead during that time wasn't...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It wasn't. The only way that you could have possibly gone ahead, and some did, they'd acquire more land or more equipment, and then when the prices broke back up to a dollar a bushel, why then, they went right ahead. They had her made.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But a guy like myself that didn't have the guts or foresight or whatever it took to do that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Didn't do it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: When it did come up, we went ahead all right after it came, but we went awful slow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't own the land to start with, right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, you didn't have anybody give you the... No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...the form to inherit it, or you didn't have the money to buy it when you were a young man, right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, that seems to put you in a different position to start with over some of these guys that had the land.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, we did finally buy that place up at Harvard, and we went...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: $5 ,000 in debt, didn't we?

speaker: Agnes Gilder
words: I don't remember. But we lost it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you buy it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: 34 minutes. 35. 34.

speaker: Agnes Gilder
words: I think. I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd just been married a pretty short time then, right?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: When you got married. Well, wheat was 30 cents then on the way up.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Got up to 60 cents finally and still wasn't enough. Because we were back on payments and we were back on interest, our taxes were kept up, but...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You couldn't pay anybody if you owed somebody. You couldn't pay them, so the only thing to do was just not owe them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Go without.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did you keep that place?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Until 1939.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, 1939. We had a walk and papers and then we came over here.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Still hard sledding. It wasn't easy going.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I think wheat was 75 cents then.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I think cream was what?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: About... clear up to 15 cents a pound. 11. 11 cents. 11 cents. Well, 11 cents for some of it, but I think it kind of gained a little by the time we got here. Then the war came on.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But even that didn't make any difference to us for...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: oh, what?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It took two or three years for that to affect us here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Very much.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Didn't affect anybody, but the ones that had it already paid for. It took a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why? What was their advantage?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Price didn't come up. Price didn't come up for three years after the war. Slow starting, but they did eventually start.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: During the war all the neighbors around here pulled out and went to work for... ...for war effort.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of people did that, huh?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, you bet.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Shultons, Telfries, Davises, Fountains, Basins.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they go to Portland mostly? Seattle? Did they go to Portland mostly?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, no. They went to Bayview. They went to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: every place.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Any place there was war efforts. One of them went to work up there at the hospital, construction work.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Longview and the ship character and Boeing were the main...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They take their families or just... Most of them, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...who kept their places, though?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Some of them didn't. Some of them... Most of them didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Some of them were so badly indebted, weren't ever going to come out of it anyway, so they just... ...all the way out of here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was right around here? Right on the creek here?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, right here. Well, Allen's Place, the Muellys, Fountains, and the next one beyond Anna Marie's.

speaker: Sam Schrager

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They helped.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then later on the neighbor on the other side came in and he helped. They didn't share and I did mine. But even so, none of us had money enough to work with.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Because there was no wire, there were no posts, there was nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did you put up the fence with? Who? What? What were the details?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you use for...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, how did I get the fence?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, it wasn't easy. I bought a lot of new wire all I could. I bought it and I'd see somebody out here that had started tractor farming and he'd taken up his fence so I'd trade him something for his old wire and then I'd bring it home and put it up and go out and see if I couldn't trade with another one. I traded a sheep,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: see some fellow down at Genesee for a trip load.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And I traded a horse for a guy down here on American Ridge for a mile of hog wire fence.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then the Anderson boys gave me enough wire for about a mile of fence for three wires.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I was lined up with the darn place. It had around seven miles of fence on it and you could turn a sheep or a hog anyplace on it and he'd stay there. It was tight.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And now I've taken it off, it's on my way. I quit the stock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At that time, had most farmers around gotten rid of their horses?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, no, there were lots of horses then.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But they started right after that to get rid of them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, but right around here close, no, there wasn't any tractors. Back a mile or two, there was a few.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But they started coming in about then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We didn't buy a tractor until about 1952 or three, did we?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they really a lot better than horses?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, those old wheel tractors weren't. No, they weren't better than horses. They were just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We couldn't plow on the hillside with ours. I don't know whether it was us or the tractor, but we never did get so we could do a good job plowing. On the steep hillside, we couldn't go up and down it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The horses would go round and round or up and down. They didn't care.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I was next to the last in the whole area to quit the horses and go to tractor Charlie Barquet down there. Hung on for a year or two after I did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The only way I could see to get a tractor was going down. I didn't want to go down. I'm going to go up.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There's three of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see any advantage at that time to get one? Was it an attractive idea to have one to get rid of the horses?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It was ideal for us because we could pull more with it. I took the horses off that. We could hurl and do things like that. But the horses filled in. Both places that were difficult or not effective with the tractor, they were effective with the horses. It was a good system and it would still be good today if we do it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: By the time we got our tractor, we had about 60 to 75 acres of hay. That was an awful job for horses. It was terrible on the horses. The mower was really something they could rake and they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was the mower hard on them? On the horses? Was it hard on them? No, why was the hay?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It was heavy and it was hot.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They hadn't done very much from the time the spring crop was in until the hay started to give them about a month layoff and they were soft.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It made a bad on them. A man felt awful bad about working them that way. When he got that mower, Lloyd mowed 20 acres back there in one day and with the horses it would have taken three.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The next day the horses went out and raked it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I still think it would be a good idea to have a good team and use them where they worked the best and forget the tractor. There are so many jobs they can do. The gas is not necessary for them. The rake and haying time are hauling it in. They are actually better than the tractor. They are not faster but they are better. As far as cost goes, there isn't any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the work steady most of the time? Or would it be sometimes on, sometimes off?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's the way it was. I couldn't go back. I didn't have a car or stay away from home on a job. I just had to take hit or miss jobs but that's fine. If I wasn't busy at the job, I was busy here.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: If you keep the financial end of it up, that wasn't necessary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you work as much in the winter or was there a lot of layoff time in the winter?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: To work off the place, the only thing you could do is you might be able to get a job skidding logs someplace.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I didn't cut wood and sold it or worked at the buildings. I didn't have to have the wood to eat on. No, it was hit or miss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you have time to sit around in the winter like now or would you be up and out working every day?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, I was working every day at something because stock required it. But I was young and husky and ambitious. I liked to do it. Oh, when the snow would get unselfably deep or it was terribly cold, I could lay off. Yeah, and I did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But it was nice working. I liked to be out of it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I had them plan or something I wanted to accomplish and that's the time to accomplish it. But right now, I couldn't see it as it being tomorrow or any other thing.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: After we got squared around and got a car with him, I could go back farther, go to Moscow or someplace to work with him. That made quite a difference too. But we didn't have a car. In order to have a car, you had to go in debt. I wasn't interested.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I didn't mind the debt if I knew that there was some source to pay it off when the time come, but I'd have that. I'd have my wings trimmed and I never got over it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About what year was it that you got the car to go?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: About 1948, I suppose. Got a hundred dollar one.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But it went and come back. It's all necessary. Sorry.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I see Carter Hart thinking about putting Harold Davis on staff or Harold Brown.
